title: Convergence Type Code Source Refactor
subtitle: Make convergence types, not templates, the executable canonical source for living-doc semantics.
scope: Living Doc Compositor convergence type registry, code definitions, template compositions, generated JSON, MCP diagnostics, skills, rendered docs, and contracts.
sourceCoverage: Created from the agreed verbatim objective after identifying that current executable semantics are template-centered while convergence types remain JSON-authored. GitHub epic #177 and subtickets #178-#184 now hold the implementation plan.
objective: Refactor Living Doc Compositor so convergence types, not templates, become the executable canonical source for living-doc semantics. Define convergence types in code, generate the registry JSON from those definitions, and make templates compose those convergence-type definitions instead of re-owning ground-level semantics. The refactor is complete when the current registry can be regenerated deterministically from code-defined convergence types, existing rendered docs and compositor flows still work, generated template semantics continue to derive from the same source, MCP diagnostics and skill context can query convergence-type contracts directly, and contract tests prevent drift between code definitions, registry JSON, templates, rendered HTML, and MCP behavior.
successCondition: Every acceptance criterion in this living doc is satisfied or explicitly blocked with a source-grounded reason; no completion claim is valid from template-code generation alone.

objectiveFacets.name: Convergence types are canonical
objectiveFacets.description: Convergence types become the executable semantic ground level instead of remaining JSON-authored vocabulary while templates own executable behavior.

objectiveFacets.name: Registry JSON generation
objectiveFacets.description: The current registry JSON can be regenerated deterministically from code-defined convergence types.

objectiveFacets.name: Templates compose type definitions
objectiveFacets.description: Templates become compositions over convergence-type definitions instead of re-owning ground-level semantics.

objectiveFacets.name: Runtime behavior preserved
objectiveFacets.description: Existing rendered docs, compositor flows, generated template semantics, and sync flows keep working during and after the refactor.

objectiveFacets.name: Inference-time access
objectiveFacets.description: MCP diagnostics and skills can query convergence-type contracts directly.

objectiveFacets.name: Drift control
objectiveFacets.description: Contract tests prevent drift between code definitions, registry JSON, templates, rendered HTML, and MCP behavior.

invariants.name: Objective wording is fixed
invariants.statement: The objective text is the agreed verbatim objective and must not be narrowed to fit completed implementation slices.

invariants.name: Convergence types are the ground level
invariants.statement: Do not treat template definitions as the canonical semantic source when the accountable objective says convergence types are the source.

invariants.name: Registry JSON becomes generated artifact
invariants.statement: The target state is not code plus hand-maintained equivalent registry JSON; registry JSON must be reproducible from code-defined convergence types.

invariants.name: Preserve public artifacts
invariants.statement: Existing JSON, rendered HTML, compositor, docs/scripts mirrors, and GitHub Pages output must continue to work unless an explicit migration and compatibility proof is recorded.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.rationale: Keeps the objective state visible: this is a new refactor living doc, not the completed template-semantics refactor.

callout.title: Generator path exists, strict closure still blocked
items: scripts/generate-living-doc-registry-from-definitions.mjs reproduces the registry in transitional mode.
--check now detects drift for code-defined convergence types; --strict fails until all current types are migrated.
The generated-registry acceptance criterion remains required until #180 removes the legacy registry-authored type surface.

sections.title: Acceptance Criteria
sections.rationale: Defines objective-tight closure gates. Each criterion preserves a phrase from the verbatim objective and states the proof needed before completion can be accepted.

data.name: Convergence types become executable canonical source
data.objectiveTerm: convergence types, not templates, become the executable canonical source for living-doc semantics
data.criterion: Every current convergence type has an executable code definition that is treated as the authored semantic source for its reusable contract.
data.proofRequired: Code definitions exist for the current convergence type registry surface, and the system no longer requires hand-authored registry JSON as the canonical source for those type contracts.
data.acceptanceTest: A reviewer can point at the code definition for any current convergence type and identify the source for its name, category, kind, projection, fields, status contract, prompt guidance, structural contract, and operational metadata.

ticketIds.title: Epic: Make convergence types the executable canonical source
Define the convergence-type code definition API
Migrate current convergence types into code definitions

notes: #178 proves the API shape and one real type fixture only.
The criterion remains required until every current convergence type has moved into code and the registry no longer depends on hand-authored JSON as canonical source.

data.name: Registry JSON regenerates deterministically
data.objectiveTerm: generate the registry JSON from those definitions
data.criterion: The committed registry JSON is generated from code-defined convergence types and can be reproduced deterministically.
data.proofRequired: A generator command writes scripts/living-doc-registry.json from convergence-type code definitions, and a check command fails when the JSON drifts from code.
data.acceptanceTest: Running the generator followed by its check leaves scripts/living-doc-registry.json, docs/scripts/living-doc-registry.json, and embedded compositor registry data up to date.

ticketIds.title: Epic: Make convergence types the executable canonical source
Generate registry JSON from convergence-type definitions

notes: #179 adds the deterministic generator/check path.
The generator is transitional: it overlays code-defined convergence types and preserves legacy registry-authored types until #180 migrates them.
The criterion remains required because the current registry is not yet fully regenerated from code-defined convergence types in strict mode.

data.name: Templates compose convergence-type definitions
data.objectiveTerm: make templates compose those convergence-type definitions instead of re-owning ground-level semantics
data.criterion: Template definitions reference and compose convergence-type contracts rather than duplicating the ground-level semantics owned by those types.
data.proofRequired: Template definition code shows composition over convergence-type definitions, with template-specific relationships and roles kept distinct from type-owned fields, statuses, structural contracts, and prompt guidance.
data.acceptanceTest: Changing a convergence-type definition flows into affected template generation without manually duplicating that change inside each template.

ticketIds.title: Epic: Make convergence types the executable canonical source
Refactor templates to compose convergence-type definitions

data.name: Rendered docs and compositor flows still work
data.objectiveTerm: existing rendered docs and compositor flows still work, generated template semantics continue to derive from the same source
data.criterion: Existing render, compositor, sync, generated template semantic, and registry overview flows remain compatible after convergence types move into code.
data.proofRequired: Contract, render, sync, and focused e2e checks pass after regenerating registry JSON, template semantic artifacts, rendered docs, and embedded compositor data.
data.acceptanceTest: The standard render/sync/test commands pass and the generated template semantic graph still derives from the code-defined convergence-type source through template composition.

ticketIds.title: Epic: Make convergence types the executable canonical source
Preserve rendered docs and compositor flows during convergence-type migration

data.name: MCP and skills query type contracts directly
data.objectiveTerm: MCP diagnostics and skill context can query convergence-type contracts directly
data.criterion: Inference-time tooling exposes convergence-type contracts as direct queryable surfaces for diagnostics and skills.
data.proofRequired: MCP tools and skill instructions can retrieve or use generated convergence-type contracts without reconstructing them from rendered prose or template-local duplication.
data.acceptanceTest: A skill can ask for a convergence type's executable contract and use it to reason about fields, status logic, structural contract, relationship participation, and valid repair behavior.

ticketIds.title: Epic: Make convergence types the executable canonical source
Expose convergence-type contracts to MCP diagnostics and skills

data.name: Contracts prevent cross-surface drift
data.objectiveTerm: contract tests prevent drift between code definitions, registry JSON, templates, rendered HTML, and MCP behavior
data.criterion: Drift between the code-defined convergence types and every generated/consuming surface is caught by tests or check commands.
data.proofRequired: Contracts cover code definitions, generated registry JSON, template compositions, generated template artifacts, rendered HTML context, compositor embeds, docs/scripts mirrors, and MCP behavior.
data.acceptanceTest: A deliberate mismatch between a convergence-type code definition and generated registry/template/MCP artifacts causes a deterministic check or contract test to fail.

ticketIds.title: Epic: Make convergence types the executable canonical source
Add drift-prevention contracts for code-defined convergence types

sections.title: Formal Model
sections.rationale: Defines the architecture distinction this refactor must preserve: convergence types are ground-level semantic atoms; templates are compositions over those atoms.

data.name: Convergence type as semantic atom
data.definition: A convergence type is the reusable semantic atom of a living doc. Its executable definition should own the type contract that the registry currently carries as JSON.
data.authorityScope: Name, category, kind, projection, fields, sources, statuses, structural contract, prompt guidance, and generated operational metadata for each convergence type.
violations: Leaving convergence types as hand-authored JSON while claiming code is the canonical semantic source.
Duplicating type-owned semantics inside template definitions.
notes: The current registry JSON is the source artifact to migrate from, not the target canonical source.
This model does not require concrete living doc instances to move out of JSON.

ticketIds.title: Epic: Make convergence types the executable canonical source
Define the convergence-type code definition API
Migrate current convergence types into code definitions

data.name: Template as composition over types
data.definition: A template is a document-shape composition over convergence types. It may define section order, section role, template-specific relationships, and stage pressures, but it must not become the ground-level type source.
data.authorityScope: Template identity, selected convergence type sequence, template-specific relationships, stage signals, valid operations, and generated template semantic artifacts.
violations: Treating templates as the canonical unit of semantic truth.
Copying field/status/prompt contracts from convergence types into every template that uses them.
notes: The previous template-definition refactor is useful proof of the derivation path, but it is not the final canonical source architecture.

ticketIds.title: Epic: Make convergence types the executable canonical source
Refactor templates to compose convergence-type definitions

sections.title: Code Anchors
sections.rationale: Pins the refactor to the current files that prove the mismatch and the future files expected to close it.

data.name: Current convergence-type registry JSON
data.what_it_does: Currently holds all convergence type definitions as JSON.
why_it_matters: This is the ground-level source today.
The objective requires this source to become generated from code-defined convergence types.

Generate registry JSON from convergence-type definitions
Migrate current convergence types into code definitions

data.name: Current executable template definitions
data.what_it_does: Defines executable semantics for template compositions.
why_it_matters: This proves code-based derivation works for template compositions.
It also shows the current mismatch: templates have executable semantics while convergence types remain JSON-authored.

ticketIds.title: Epic: Make convergence types the executable canonical source
Refactor templates to compose convergence-type definitions

data.name: Convergence-type code definition API and fixture
data.what_it_does: Defines the code-side convergence-type API, exports code-defined types, and provides acceptance-criteria as the first real proof fixture.
why_it_matters: This starts the convergence-type-first source layer rather than adding more template-owned semantics.
The fixture proves one current registry type can be represented in code without losing its authored registry contract.
The full migration is still open because the registry generator and remaining convergence-type modules are not implemented yet.

ticketIds.title: Define the convergence-type code definition API
Migrate current convergence types into code definitions

data.name: Convergence-type definition contract test
data.what_it_does: Checks that the code definition API covers the current registry field boundary, validates entity/status references, keeps generated semanticUses out of authored type definitions, and proves the acceptance-criteria fixture matches the registry.
why_it_matters: This gives #178 a deterministic proof instead of only a proposed API shape.
It also records the generated metadata boundary before registry generation is implemented.

ticketIds.title: Define the convergence-type code definition API

data.name: Registry generation from convergence-type definitions
data.what_it_does: Generates scripts/living-doc-registry.json by overlaying code-defined convergence types onto the current registry and provides --check plus --strict modes.
why_it_matters: This creates the deterministic generation path required before full migration can be proven.
The normal check catches drift for code-defined types now.
The strict check names the remaining legacy registry-authored types and must pass before the full objective can close.

ticketIds.title: Generate registry JSON from convergence-type definitions

data.name: Registry generation contract test
data.what_it_does: Runs generator --check, verifies transitional reproduction of the committed registry, proves code-defined type drift is repaired from code, and asserts strict mode fails until all types are migrated.
why_it_matters: This prevents treating the generator script as proof without executable checks.
It keeps the partial nature of #179 visible while still making the next migration step testable.

ticketIds.title: Generate registry JSON from convergence-type definitions

sections.title: Attempt Log
sections.rationale: Tracks implementation attempts without letting a useful slice become a false completion claim.

data.name: Crystallize the real objective after template-centered drift
what_tried: Recorded the verbatim objective selected in conversation.
Added an acceptance-criteria section using the new convergence type.
Separated current template-code proof from the deeper convergence-type-code objective.
Created GitHub epic #177 and implementation subtickets #178-#184.
Mapped each subticket back to the objective-bound acceptance criteria.
what_proved: The objective is now explicit enough that template-level code generation alone cannot satisfy it.
The living doc has closure gates before implementation begins.
The implementation plan now has a source-system spine instead of remaining only in prose.

data.name: Define the convergence-type code API and first fixture
what_tried: Covered the authored registry field boundary, including card-grid and edge-table shapes, status fields, entity sources, AI actions/profiles, column headers, edge notes, and nestable flags.
Made generatedFields explicit so generated semanticUses is not authored into convergence-type modules.
Added scripts/living-doc-definitions/convergence-types/acceptance-criteria.mjs as the first real type fixture.
Added tests/contract/convergence-type-definitions.spec.mjs and included it in npm run test:contract.
what_proved: A real current convergence type can be represented in code and compared to its registry entry without semantic loss.
#178 has a concrete API surface and validation path.
The full objective remains open because registry generation, full migration, template composition, MCP exposure, and cross-surface drift tests are not complete.

ticketIds.title: Define the convergence-type code definition API

data.name: Add transitional registry generator and check mode
what_tried: Made the generator overlay code-defined convergence type entries while preserving current entityTypes, statusSets, legacy convergence types, and generated fields.
Added tests/contract/convergence-type-registry-generation.spec.mjs and included it in npm run test:contract.
Ran the generator once and synced compositor embeds so registry mirrors match the generated order.
what_proved: The registry can now be reproduced deterministically for the current code-defined type surface.
Drift in a migrated convergence type is repaired from code rather than accepted from hand-edited registry JSON.
Strict full generation is not yet achieved because 47 current convergence types still need code definitions under #180.

sections.rationale: Lists current and target commands that will operate the code-defined convergence-type source and its generated artifacts.

data.name: Current template generation chain
data.purpose: Generate current template semantic artifacts and generated registry semantic-use annotations.
data.whenToUse: Use as the baseline proof that generation already works above the convergence-type layer.
data.invocation: node scripts/generate-living-doc-semantic-graph.mjs && node scripts/generate-living-doc-semantic-diagrams.mjs && node scripts/generate-living-doc-template-artifacts.mjs && node scripts/generate-living-doc-registry-semantics.mjs && node scripts/sync-compositor-embeds.mjs
caveats: This chain does not generate the registry JSON from code-defined convergence types yet.

ticketIds.title: Refactor templates to compose convergence-type definitions

data.name: Registry generation from convergence-type definitions
data.purpose: Generate registry JSON from the code-defined convergence types that have been migrated, while preserving legacy registry-authored types until #180 is complete.
data.whenToUse: Run before sync when a code-defined convergence type changes; use --check in tests and --strict only after all current types have code definitions.
caveats: Transitional mode is not full objective closure because most convergence types are still legacy-authored in registry JSON.
Strict mode is the target closure check for #180 and currently fails by design.
